facts.claim: OSPF process 1: networks include 10.0.0.0/30 area 0 (observed on R1 and R2 configs)

root_causes.cause: R2 is not advertising its LAN (172.16.0.0/24) into OSPF, so R1 has no learned route to 172.16.0.11. As a result, packets from 192.168.0.11 are not routed to the 172.16.0.0/24 network.
evidence: R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/0 configured as 172.16.0.1/24 (R2 config).
R2 OSPF process networks: listed 10.0.0.0 and 10.0.0.4 (no 172.16.0.0 network statement) — R2 ospf config shows only "10.0.0.0" and "10.0.0.4" under networks.

R1 OSPF process networks include 192.168.0.0 and the transit 10.0.0.0 network (R1 ospf includes "10.0.0.0" and "192.168.0.0"); R1 has no 'routing' section (null), indicating it relies on OSPF to learn remote subnets.

data.intent_description: R2 is not advertising 172.16.0.0/24 into OSPF, so R1 has no route to 172.16.0.11; restore OSPF advertisement of the 172.16.0.0/24 LAN so 192.168.0.11 can reach 172.16.0.11.

plan.problem: R2 is not advertising its 172.16.0.0/24 LAN into OSPF; remote hosts cannot be reached from 192.168.0.11 because R1 has no learned route to 172.16.0.0/24.
plan.fix_summary: Configure R2 to advertise 172.16.0.0/24 into OSPF by adding the appropriate network statement (area 0) so R1 learns the route to 172.16.0.11.

facts.claim: OSPF process 1: networks include 10.0.0.0/30 area 0 (observed on R1 and R2 configs)

evidence: get_ospf(router2) now shows GigabitEthernet0/0/0 in the OSPF process (interface present in ospf_full).
get_interface_status(router2 Gi0/0/0) shows admin_state=up and oper_state=up.
ping from router1 to 172.16.0.11: Success rate is 100 percent (5/5), round-trip min/avg/max = 3/3/4 ms — demonstrates R1 can reach the 172.16.0.11 host.

facts.claim: OSPF process 1: networks include 10.0.0.0/30 area 0 (observed on R1 and R2 configs)

root_causes.cause: R2 is not advertising its LAN (172.16.0.0/24) into OSPF, so R1 has no learned route to 172.16.0.11. As a result, packets from 192.168.0.11 are not routed to the 172.16.0.0/24 network.
evidence: R2 GigabitEthernet0/0/0 configured as 172.16.0.1/24 (R2 config).
R2 OSPF process networks: listed 10.0.0.0 and 10.0.0.4 (no 172.16.0.0 network statement) — R2 ospf config shows only "10.0.0.0" and "10.0.0.4" under networks.

R1 OSPF process networks include 192.168.0.0 and the transit 10.0.0.0 network (R1 ospf includes "10.0.0.0" and "192.168.0.0"); R1 has no 'routing' section (null), indicating it relies on OSPF to learn remote subnets.

plan.problem: R2 is not advertising its 172.16.0.0/24 LAN into OSPF; remote hosts cannot be reached from 192.168.0.11 because R1 has no learned route to 172.16.0.0/24.
plan.fix_summary: Configure R2 to advertise 172.16.0.0/24 into OSPF by adding the appropriate network statement (area 0) so R1 learns the route to 172.16.0.11.

evidence: get_ospf(router2) now shows GigabitEthernet0/0/0 in the OSPF process (interface present in ospf_full).
get_interface_status(router2 Gi0/0/0) shows admin_state=up and oper_state=up.
ping from router1 to 172.16.0.11: Success rate is 100 percent (5/5), round-trip min/avg/max = 3/3/4 ms — demonstrates R1 can reach the 172.16.0.11 host.

messages.content:
What was observed
- R1 had no route to 172.16.0.11 because R2‘s LAN (172.16.0.0/24 on Gi0/0/0) was not being advertised into OSPF.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- R2 OSPF process lacked a network statement for 172.16.0.0/24, so the subnet was not present in the OSPF LSDB and R1 could not learn it.

Changes made
- On R2, added OSPF process 1 network statement for 172.16.0.0 0.0.0.255 area 0 (advertising 172.16.0.0/24 into OSPF).

Verification
- Passed. Evidence: R2 now shows Gi0/0/0 in the OSPF process (interface in ospf_full), Gi0/0/0 is up/up, and ping from R1 to 172.16.0.11 succeeded (5/5, 3–4 ms).

Remaining items
- None. The 172.16.0.0/24 network is now advertised and reachable.

data.last_message:
What was observed
- R1 had no route to 172.16.0.11 because R2‘s LAN (172.16.0.0/24 on Gi0/0/0) was not being advertised into OSPF.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- R2 OSPF process lacked a network statement for 172.16.0.0/24, so the subnet was not present in the OSPF LSDB and R1 could not learn it.

Changes made
- On R2, added OSPF process 1 network statement for 172.16.0.0 0.0.0.255 area 0 (advertising 172.16.0.0/24 into OSPF).

Verification
- Passed. Evidence: R2 now shows Gi0/0/0 in the OSPF process (interface in ospf_full), Gi0/0/0 is up/up, and ping from R1 to 172.16.0.11 succeeded (5/5, 3–4 ms).

Remaining items
- None. The 172.16.0.0/24 network is now advertised and reachable.
